Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but I often found myself drifting away from the plan. I'd sit down with good intentions, maybe watch some videos or flip through materials, but I wasn't super consistent about it. With 25 hours a week supposedly dedicated to studying, a lot of that time was me staring at things without really absorbing much. I'm a visual learner, so I'd make diagrams and charts, but organizing when to actually do that was always a struggle.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity when deadlines were approaching, then kind of coast for a while after. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be scrambling. It wasn't really by choice—life stuff and my extracurricular commitments would pull me in different directions. I always meant to be more regular about it, but that rarely happened.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing something important, that kind of scared feeling would push me to look. Other times it was more random—I'd remember the course existed and feel guilty enough to poke around. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me curious a few times, so I'd log in to see what those were about.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially early in the semester when everything was new. But it never really stuck as a habit for me. Once the novelty wore off, I'd forget about the LMS for days at a time. The gamified elements helped a bit—earning badges or seeing progress bars made me want to check in more—but even that faded eventually. I just couldn't make it feel natural.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I liked being able to pause and rewatch parts that didn't click the first time. The diagrams and visual summaries some instructors included were gold. Discussion posts were okay too—seeing how other students understood things helped me figure out if I was on the right track. Anything that let me see information rather than just read dense text worked better for my brain.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading-heavy resources and long articles. Without reliable internet access, downloading those was a hassle, and honestly, walls of text just don't stick for me. I'd skim them at best. Some of the optional quizzes I'd skip too, figuring I'd get to them later—which usually meant never. If something looked boring or overwhelming at first glance, I'd push it aside and tell myself I'd circle back.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or if it connected clearly to what would be on exams. The gamified tasks were actually pretty good at this—I could see myself leveling up or completing challenges, which gave me a little dopamine hit. If something felt abstract or disconnected from my grades, I struggled to justify spending time on it. Visuals and interactivity made a huge difference in whether I'd actually engage.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them or face consequences. With optional stuff, even if it might help me learn, the lack of urgency meant I'd push it aside for things that felt more pressing. The gamified optional tasks were a bit different because they felt more like games than work, so I was more willing to try those. But generally, optional meant 'probably not happening.'
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials when I could get internet access, then work through them later at home. I made tons of handwritten notes and visual maps that I'd study from repeatedly. I also used some textbooks I borrowed and YouTube videos for topics I didn't understand. The LMS wouldn't show any of that—just me watching a video once, not the hours I spent reviewing my notes afterward.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be deep into studying from my printed notes or hand-drawn diagrams, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours going over material I'd already downloaded, making connections between concepts visually. Sometimes I'd study with friends from my extracurricular activities, quizzing each other verbally. None of that left any digital trace. My actual learning often happened completely disconnected from the platform.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it if possible, or take detailed visual notes, and then never go back to the original. My notes became my primary study material. I'd review those for hours without ever logging back in. It was more practical for me given my internet situation, and honestly, I preferred studying from my own organized summaries anyway.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches where engaging felt impossible. When I got scared about falling behind or not understanding something, I'd sometimes freeze up instead of pushing through. Midway through the semester, I had a lot going on with my extracurricular commitments, and the course just slipped down my priority list. It wasn't that I didn't care—I was just overwhelmed and didn't know where to start catching up.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. After long days with my extracurricular activities, I'd be too drained to focus properly on coursework. Without consistent internet, I had to plan when I could even access the LMS, which added another layer of difficulty. When assignments piled up, I'd get this paralyzed feeling where I didn't know what to tackle first, so sometimes I'd tackle nothing. The competing responsibilities definitely shaped when and how I could engage.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement in the system, but I wasn't really learning anything meaningful during them.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was that scared feeling—I'd worry I couldn't do them well enough, so I'd avoid starting. Other times, I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure and put things off until the last minute. Looking at my assignment completion rate, it clearly didn't work out great. The fear of not being good enough sometimes kept me from trying at all.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving sometimes. I'd have these intense bursts of LMS activity right before something was due—watching videos, checking requirements, scrambling to understand material. Without a deadline, I'd drift. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created mini-deadlines and goals, but traditional assignment deadlines were still the main driver of my engagement patterns.
Q17: When I got good feedback, especially on assignments where I scored well, it motivated me to keep going. Seeing that 93% on assignment scores made me feel like maybe I wasn't as lost as I thought. But feedback that was vague or delayed didn't really help—by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. Quick, specific feedback made me want to engage more; slow or generic feedback just felt like noise.
Q18: The structure was a mixed bag for me. Having everything organized in the LMS was helpful for knowing what existed, but the pacing sometimes felt off for my life. When modules were released in ways that didn't match my available time, I'd fall behind. The gamified elements added some structure I actually enjoyed—clear goals, visible progress. But overall, I needed more flexibility than the course structure really allowed.
Q19: The visual materials and gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they matched how I learn. But the heavy reading requirements and some of the dense text-based resources were barriers for me. Not having reliable internet made everything harder too; the course seemed designed assuming everyone had constant access. More downloadable, visual content would have helped me a lot. The discussion forums were okay but felt forced sometimes.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—exploring the platform, watching videos, checking things out, but never quite getting to the submission part. Fear of not doing well enough, or getting distracted before finishing, or just running out of time before deadlines. High activity might mean genuine interest or curiosity, but something's blocking the final step of actually turning work in. It's not always laziness; sometimes it's anxiety or life circumstances.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my situation. My exam scores and assignment quality were decent when I did submit, but my LMS activity was pretty moderate. I did a lot of learning offline—from my notes, downloaded materials, studying with friends. The platform only captures part of the picture. Someone can understand the material well without constantly logging in, especially if they're studying in ways the system can't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement completely. They can't tell if you're actually absorbing information or just staring blankly at a screen. They miss all the offline studying, the discussions with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the computer. It's like measuring only the visible tip of an iceberg.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to check boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, it often looks like creating visual maps and really thinking through how ideas relate. It's not about time spent logged in—it's about mental investment. Some of my most engaged moments happened nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS would never show how scared I sometimes felt about the course, or how that fear both motivated and paralyzed me. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with my handwritten notes and diagrams, or the conversations with friends that helped concepts click. It can't measure my actual understanding or the effort I put into learning in my own way. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the data they were collecting.
